macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishna: Objects That Must Be Announced (Elu Chayav Lehachriz)

micro_segments.title: Opening and parallel to Elu Metziot: fruit in vessels and coins in wallets
The role of simanim (identifying characteristics) in determining ownership
Examples from the mishna: fruit in vessels, coins in wallets, bundles of straw, homeowner's loaves, wool shearings, jars of wine and oil

macro_segments.title: The Case of Fruits Next to a Container: Reconciling Two Braitot on Partial Containment

micro_segments.title: The gemara's initial question on the reasoning for chayav lehachriz
First braita: fruits/money next to vessel means you must announce; partially inside means you must announce
Second braita: when owner claims the vessel, finder can keep the fruits—apparent contradiction
Rav Zevid's resolution: difference between flax and fruit based on spillage likelihood
Alternative explanations from Rav Pappa regarding container contents and orientation
Final resolution through distinctions of container design and lips

macro_segments.title: Piles of Fruit and Coins (Tziburei Peirot and Tziburei Ma'ot): Number and Location as Simanim

micro_segments.title: Question of whether the plural form teaches that both number and location serve as simanim

macro_segments.title: Three Coins Stacked: Definition of 'Like a Tower' (Kemigdalin)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yitzchak Migdala's definition: coins stacked like a tower
Braita's distinction: scattered coins are yours, but coins in tower form must be announced
Question of what 'tower' means and the tanna's clarification that only perfectly stacked counts as non-scattered
Rabbi Chanina's restriction: tower arrangement requires three different denominations of coins
Explanation of Rabbi Chanina's position through different coin sizes as simanim
Rabbi Yochanan's dispute: even same-denomination coins in tower form require announcement
Ravina's limitation: quantity must be significant enough to announce (teiva machriz)
Rav Yirmiyah's extended question: various stacking arrangements (circle, line, triangle, ladder)
Rav Nachman's criterion: whether coins can be lifted together as a unit
Rav Ashi's imagery: coins like stones of Beis Kulus (tower-like stacking)
Braita's additional configuration: Beis Kulus arrangement (coins flanking sides and one on top)

macro_segments.title: Coins Have No Siman: The Fungibility Principle

micro_segments.title: Braita on coin claims: new coins, coins with ruler's face, specific ruler's coins are not simanim
Reason: coins are highly fungible and change hands frequently, unlike vessels
The presumption that a claimed coin was already spent and returned to circulation

macro_segments.title: Birds Tied Together Behind Fences and Objects in Trash Piles: Safek Hinuach

micro_segments.title: Mishna: found birds tied behind fence or wall, or in field paths—do not touch them
Reasoning: location as siman (safek hinuach—uncertain if placed intentionally)
Challenge: why isn't the knot in the rope binding the birds a siman?
Rav Abba bar Zavda and Rav Ukva bar Chama: birds tied by wings in standard manner, location as makom siman
Rav Abba bar Zavda's ruling on safek hinuach: don't take initially, but if taken cannot return
Mishna on vessels in trash: covered—do not touch; uncovered—take and announce
Braita contradiction: hidden vessel in trash should be takeable because trash is regularly removed
Rav Zevid's resolution: distinction based on type of vessel (large vs. small keilim)
Alternative resolution: distinction between trash pile that is regularly cleaned vs. not cleaned
Discussion of owner's intention (yeush mida'at) when throwing items in trash

macro_segments.title: Objects in Walls and Rock Piles: Ownership Based on Location and Wall Age

micro_segments.title: Mishna: objects found in pile of rocks or old wall can be kept; new wall objects have midpoint rule
Explanation: new walls have hidey-holes; halfway out belongs to finder, halfway in belongs to householder
Rental property exception: even objects inside belong to finder because prior occupant is unknown
Gemara's question: why attribute old wall finds to Canaanites rather than Israelites?
Gemara's resolution: objects in old walls must be deeply buried or containerized to claim they were placed

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Chanina vs. Rabbi Yochanan on same-denomination coins
Rav Zevid's flax vs. fruit distinction
Kotel chadash vs. kotel yashan (new vs. old wall)
Rav Ashi's criterion: lifting coins together
